text: CATTARO, a small seaport of Austria, on the innermost reach of the Bocca or gulf of C., in the district of Montenegro, in 42° 25' 26", E long. 18° 46', [Carta del m. Adratica,] 7 leagues SE \(\frac{1}{2}\) S of Ragusa. It is defended by a castle and strong walls, and is overhung by Mount Sella, and the great chain of Montenegro. The streets are narrow and gloomy. Pop. 2,125. It is the see of a bishop, a suffragan of Zara. The harbour is one of the best on the Adri- See Bocca DI C. The water close to shore is atic. deep; oak timber of the best quality abounds immediately behind it; and the place is altogether admirably suited for a great naval arsenal. In former times C. possessed 450 vessels, which carried the oils, wines, and dried fruits produced along the coasts of the Adriatic to various ports of Europe; at present its trade is chiefly confined to the exportation of corn, cheese, and smoked and salted meat from the Montenegro territory, and tobacco and pitch from Albania, to Trieste and the Morea. The commercial relations of the Montenegrines are limited to C., and they bring to its market, in addition to the articles just enumerated, butter, wool, silk, charcoal, and poultry, receiving in return arms of every description, ammunition, and articles of furniture and dress. The road from C. to Cettigny, the chief residence of the metropolitan or bishop-chief of the Montenegrines, lies between precipices and ravines, amidst which, here and there, are seen vineyards, small orchards, and fields of corn.—C. was ceded to the Austrians at the peace of Campo-Formio; and afterwards to the French at the peace of Presburg in 1805. The Russians subsequently got possession of it, by the aid of the Montenegrines, and held it till the peace of Tilsit, in 1807, when it was given up to France. At the congress of Vienna, it was restored to Austria.

CATTEGAT (THE), an extensive channel or strait between Denmark and Sweden, communicating on the N with the North sea by the Skager-Rack, and on the S with the Baltic by the Great and the Little Belts, and the Sound. Its N entrance lies between the Skagen or N point of Jutland, on the W, and the SW point of Hisingen island, off the Swedish coast, on the E; both points lying nearly on the parallel of 57° 45' N. Its length CAT from N to S, measured on the meridian of 11° E, to the peninsula of Aagerup in Zealand, is 137 m.; its greatest breadth, on the parallel of 56° 35', is 97 m.; at its N entrance, 40 m. It contains the islands of Læssoe, Anholdt, and Samsoe, and a number of islets. The current in the C. is generally found setting in the direction of the wind.

CATTENOM, or Kettenhowen, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of the Moselle, arrond. of Thionville. The cant. comprises 28 com, and contained in 1831, 17,583 inhabitants; in 1841, 17,675.—The town is 6 m. NE of Thionville, on the 1. bank of the Moselle. Pop. 1,115. It was formerly surrounded with walls, and a place of considerable importance. Extensive cattle-fairs are held here annually.
CATTERALL, a township in the p. and 11 m. S of Garstang, Lancashire, at the confluence of the West Calder with the Wyse, and near the Lancaster canal. Area 1,490 acres. Pop. in 1841, 1,102.
CATTERICK, a parish of the N. R. of Yorkshire, 5½ m. SE of Richmond, on the Swale. Area 21,680 acres. Pop. in 1841, 2,965.—Also a township in the above parish. Area 1,570 acres. Pop. 600.
CATTERLIN, a township in the p. of Newton- Rigny, Cumberland, 3 m. NNW of Penrith, and near the Petterill. Pop. in 1841, 147.
CATTERTON, a township in the p. and 3 m. NE of Tadcaster. Area 770 acres. Pop. in 1841, 58.
CATTHORPE, a parish of Leicestershire. Area 520 acres. Pop. in 1841, 167.
CATTILLON - SUR - SAMBRE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Nord, cant. of Cateau- Cambrésis. Pop. in 1836, 3,120.
CATTISTOCK, a parish of Dorset, 9 m. NW of Dorchester, on the Frome. Area 2,620 acres. Pop. in 1841, 549.

CATTOLICA, a town in the States-of-the-Church, in the legation and 40 m. SE of Forli, and 8 m. NW of Rimini, on a hill near the shore of the Adriatic.— Also a town of Sicily, cap. of a circondario, in the district and 15 m. NW of Girgenti. Pop. 7,060. In the environs are extensive sulphur-works.

CATTON, a township in the p. of Croxhall, Derbyshire, 6 m. SSW of Burton-upon-Trent, on the E bank of the Trent, and on the Birmingham and Derby railway. Pop. in 1841, 47.—Also a parish of Norfolk, 3 m. N of Norwich, E of the Wensum. Area 760 acres. Pop. 650.—Also a grieveship in the p. of Allendale, Northumberland. Pop. 535 .- Also a parish in the E. R. of Yorkshire, 7 m. NE of York, on the E bank of the Derwent. Area 8,150 acres. Pop. 1,078.—Also a township in the p. of Topcliffe, N. R. of Yorkshire, 5 m. SW of Thirsk, on the E bank of the Swale. Area 770 acres. Pop. 136.
CATTON (High), a township in the p. of Catton, E. R. of Yorkshire, 8 m. S of York. Area 1,640 acres. Pop. in 1841, 185.
CATTON (Low), a township in the p. of Catton, E. R. of Yorkshire. Area 2,140 acres. Pop. in 1841, 176.
CATTYWAR. See KATTIWAR.
CATU', a village of Brazil, in the prov. of Bahia, district of São-Francisco.—Also a village in the same prov., on an affluent of the bay of Todos-os-Santos.

CATU'A, a small river of Brazil, in the prov. of Para, district of Rio-Negro, which falls into the Amazon, 30 m. above the confluence of the Cuari or Coary. Its banks abound with trees of immense magnitude; and are inhabited by Indians, who obtain subsistence by hunting, fishing, and the culture of a sweet species of potato.

CATUA'MA, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Pernambuco, on the N coast of the island of Itamaraca, at the entrance of the channel which separates the island from the continent. Pop. 300. The channel forms an excellent and much-frequented harbour, and is defended by a fort.—Also a river which forms a boundary-line between the prov. of Algoas and Pernambuco, and falls into the above-named channel.

CATULEZ, a mountain-ridge of Brazil, in the prov. of Bahia, extending NE from the Serra-do- Pingo over a distance of 35 m.—Also a town on the ridge of the same name, 30 m. N of P.-de-Contas. The inhabitants occupy themselves chiefly in agriculture and the rearing of cattle.

CATUMBELA, a river of Lower Guinea, in Benguela, which takes its rise 27 m. NNE of Caconda, unites with the Valombo, and after a total course of 300 m. in a generally WNW direction falls into the Atlantic 20 m. NNE of San-Philip-de-Benguela.
CATUNDUBA, an island of Brazil, in the group which lies near the entrance of the bay of Rio-de- Janeiro.

CATUS, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Lot, arrond. of Cahors. The cant. comprises 15 com. Pop. in 1831, 10,337; in 1841, 10,826.—The town is 11 m. NW of Cahors, on the r. bank of the Vert. Pop. 1,438.
CATWICK, a parish in the E. R. of Yorkshire, 8 m. ENE of Beverley. Area 1,650 acres. Pop. in 1841, 191.

CATWICK (GREAT), an island in the China sea, 120 m. E by S of Cape St. James, on the coast of Cochin China, in N lat. 10° 7', E long. 108° 52'.— The Little C. lies 9 m. to the SE of the greater island.
CATWORTH (GREAT), a parish of Huntingdonshire, 5 m. NNW of Kimbolton. Area 2,090 acres. Pop. in 1841, 637.
CATWORTH (LITTLE), a chapelry in the p. of Stow, Huntingdonshire. Pop. in 1841, 75.
CAUANAS, a district of Brazilian Guayana, near the source of the Cuari or Coary.
CAUBUL. See CABUL.

CAUCA, an old department of the republic of New Granada, comprising the valleys of the Atrato, the San Juan, and the Upper Cauca, or the W section of the republic stretching along the Pacific on the W, from the bay of Panama to the frontiers of Ecuador. Its area has been estimated at 68,300 sq. m.; and its pop. in 1837, at 190,000. It is divided into the 4 provinces of Choco, Buenaventura, Popayan, and Pasto, so named from their respective capitals. The cap. of the dep. is Popayan.

CAUCA, a large river in the above prov., which rises in the Paramo-de-Guanacas; runs W for 60 m. through a narrow valley, and passes near Popayan; then turns N, and flows through a magnificent valley nearly 200 m. in length, and in some places exceeding 40 m. in breadth, and in the N extremity of which, at Cartago, it has an elevation of 3,000 ft. above sea-level. At a point 30 m. N of Cartago, it enters a narrow defile, and falls, in a succession of rapids and cataracts between the Salto-de-San-Antonio and the Boca-de-Espiritu-Santo, a distance of 120 m., upwards of 2,500 ft. Emerging from this into a wider valley, it flows past the town of Antioquia and joins the Rio-Magdalena, from which its upper valley is separated by the Central Andes, on the l. bank, at the village of Ponto, 20 leagues below Mompox. Its entire course exceeds 600 m.—Its principal tributaries on the r. are the Vinagre, Amaime, Buga - la - Grande, Salamina, Buey, and and the only tributary of any importance, the Riothe Magdalena. Below the junction of its principal tributary on the r., the Nechi, it inosculates by the Lobo canal with the Magdalena. The western chain of the Andes divides the valley of the C. from the CAU Magdalena presents a grand spectacle. The two majestic streams, each flowing with a rapid current, appear to contend with each other for the superiority; and it is not till after a distance of several leagues that the clearer stream of the C. is ultimately lost in the more muddy waters of the Magdalena. The distance from Barranca at the mouth of the Magdalena, to the v. of Pinto at the confluence of the two great rivers is 261 leagues.
CAUCAHUE, an island of Chile, in a bay of the same name, in the gulf of Ancud, on the NE side of the island of Chiloe. It rises to an alt. of 280 ft.

CAUCASIAN PROVINCES, an administrative and military government of Russia, comprising, 1st, the provinces of the Caucasus proper; and, 2d, what are called the Trans-Caucasian territories: viz., 1. the provinces of Georgia; 2. Imeritia, with the principalities of Mingrelia, Guria, and Abasia; 3. the Mussulman provinces of Karabak, Shekinsk, Shirvan, Daghistan, and Talishin; 4. Armenia; and, 5. the pashalik of Akhalzike. The different Trans- Caucasian territories of Russia will be found described in this work under their respective heads; the provinces of Caucasus proper will be noticed here, after a slight sketch of the growth of the Transcaucasian dominions of Russia. In 1722 Peter the Great invaded the Persian provinces on the W shores of the Caspian, and took Taiku, Baku, and Derbend, whereupon the shah ceded to Russia the provinces of Daghistan, Shirvan, Gilan, Mazanderan, and Astrabad. The successors of Peter the Great, however, restored all these provinces in 1732. In 1769, Heraclius, king of Georgia, joined a Russian expedition sent to assist the king of Imeritia against the Turks; and, having thus compromised himself with his powerful neighbours, was obliged in self-defence to enter into a formal treaty with Catharine II. in 1783, by which he acknowledged himself a vassal of Russia. In 1801, Georgia was declared a Russian province; in 1803, the khanate of Ganja, or district of Elizabethpol, was taken possession of by Russia; and in 1805, Imeritia also was incorporated with the Czar's dominions. By the treaty of Gulistan, in 1813, Persia ceded to Russia the khanates of Sheki, Shirvan, Karabagh, Talish, Baku, Kuba, and Derbend. The treaty of Turkmanshai, concluded with Persia in 1828, gave Russia the Armenian provinces of Erivan, Nakshivan, and Ordubat; and advanced her frontier to the Araxes. The treaty of Adrianople, in 1829, added the pashalik of Akhalzike to the Russian territory. All these provinces and districts are now administered by Russian authorities; while Russia claims the right of protection over Mingrelia, Abasia, the Lesghian tribes, and Daghistan. In an interesting resume of Legkobeetaff's Obozvenie Rossiyskich Vladenii za Kawkazom, or 'Survey of the Russian possessions beyond the Caucasus' [Petersburg, 1836, 4 vols. 8vo.], given in the British and Foreign Review [vol. vii.], the following approximate estimate is given of the pop. of the Transcaucasian provinces: Males. Houses. The revenue which Russia draws from her Transcaucasian provinces is about £200,000.

It is bounded on the NW by the sea of Azof; on the N by the territory of the Don Cossacks and the prov. of Astrakhan; the river Kugoi- Jeia and Lake Bolchoillmen separating it from the former; the Manytch, and the Kuma, separating it from the latter; on the E it has the gulf of Kuma in the Caspian sea; on the S, the prov. of Georgia, from which it is separated by the Terek; and by the watershed of the rivers flowing N to the Kuban, and S to the Rioni and the Black sea. It is comprised between the parallels of 42° 55' and 46° 40' N; and the meridians of 37° and 47° E. Measured from the mouth of the Terek to that of the Kuban, along a line running ESE and WNW, its length is nearly 600 m.; along the meridian of 42°, which nearly divides it into two equal portions, its breadth is 250 m. By Guibert its area is estimated at 10,575,000 hectares; by Arssenjew, at 94,707 square versts, or 41,556 English sq. m. This last admeasurement does not include the Tchernomorsk Cossack territories. In the article Russia in the Penny Cyclopædia the area of Caucasia is estimated at 40,000 sq. m.; and in the table given in Macgregor's Commercial Tariff of Russia, at 375,000 sq. m.! while the pop. is estimated in the former at 150,000; and in the latter at only 110,000. Such are the discrepancies existing on the subject of the extent of the Russian territories, and their pop., chiefly in consequence of the vague application of names. Thus, too, while Guibert states the pop. of C. in 1835 at 125,000, the Almanach de Gotha, on the authority of M. de Keeppen, estimates the pop. at 402,800. Adopting Arssenjew's estimate of the superficies, and M. de Koppen's estimate of the pop., the density of the pop. seems to be about 10 to the sq. m., while that for all Russia is about 20. The N and W parts of this prov. generally present a level surface, being in fact prolongations of the great Astrakhan steppes. The S part is covered with a portion of the great Caucasian chain described in the next article. With the exception of its frontier-rivers, already noticed, it has few streams rising in the interior; but its lakes and salt-marshes are numerous. The climate is hot, and in many places insalubrious. Wood is rare, except on the outlying ridges of the Caucasus; and the only spots of highly fertile soil are found along the banks of the rivers, and produce rice, maize, barley, wheat, millet, sesame, tobacco, cotton, lint, silk, and wine. On the rich and extensive steppe-pastures immense herds of cattle, goats, and horses are reared. In 1830, the number of the pop. engaged in agriculture was returned at 103,884. The number of sheep held by them was 2,000,000. The chief articles of export are salt, cattle, horses, hides, caviare, and brandy.—The prov. is administered by a military and a civil governor, both of whom are under the superintendence of the governor-general of the Caucasian provinces. The civil pop. comprises about one-half of the inhabitants; about one-fourth is composed of the Cossacks of the military line along the Caucasus and the Cossacks of the Black sea; and the remainder are nomade Calmucks, Turkomans, and Nogay Tartars. Exclusive of the Cossacks of the Black sea, or the Tchernomorsk Cossacks, whose cap. is Ekaterinodar, the prov. is divided into the 4 districts of Stavropol, Georgiefsk, Kizliar, and Mozdok. From Kislar, at the mouth of the Terek, to Mozdok, and thence to Georgiefsk, there extends a line of forts and fortified posts which joins the Kuban at Batal-Pachinskoi, 65 m. to the W of Georgiefsk, and runs down that river to Labinskaia. 120 m. WNW of Stavropol. The defence of this line is intrusted to the Cossacks of the Line, as they are II. * called. From Labinskaia to the mouth of the Kuban, the defence is intrusted to the Cossacks of the Black sea. All the mountain-territory lying to the SW of this line, with the exception of a few fortresses on the coast of the Black sea, is still in the hands of the Circassian tribes. From Mozdok, or rather from Ekaterinograd higher up the Terek, a great military line of road is carried up the valley of the Terek through Kabardah, and across the Kazbek, by the pass of Dariel, to Teffis. The main road intersecting the prov. runs from Kislar, up the Terek, to Mozdok; thence to Georgiefsk; and thence to Stavropol, where it parts into two branches, one running NNW to Nakshivan, and the other W, along the N bank of the Kuban to the straits of Jenikaleh. Another steppe-road leads directly N from Mozdok, across the Kuma and the Manytch, into the prov. of Astrakhan. To effect and maintain some amount of control over the Circassian tribes occupying the Western Caucasus, it was proposed by Paskiewitch to carry a line of occupation from Ghelendik on the Black sea to the lower part of the Kuban; and another line from a point farther to the E, on the Abasian coast, over the Caucasus, by the Col-de-Jumatan, to the mineral springs in the valleys of the Elburz; but both these designs have been utterly baffled by the intrepidity of the Circassians, who repulsed the Russian general Williamoff, and captured or destroyed the new forts he had established between Olginsky on the Kuban and Pshad on the Black sea. The strength of the Russian army in the C. varies much from year to year with the rate of mortality and the arrival of reinforcements. In 1843, it amounted to 117,000 men, a number by no means disproportionate to the extent of its line of operations. This force it is reported suffers dreadfully from fever, which in some years carries off a sixth of the whole army.

CAU'CASUS, an extensive mountain-system extending, like an immense wall, across the isthmus between the Black sea and the Caspian, or from the mouth of the Kuban in 44° 50' N lat. and 37° 10' E long., on the NW, to Cape Apcheron on the Caspian, in 40° 21' N lat. and 49° 40' E long., on the SE. Although the isthmus of the C. forms a kind of connecting link betwixt Europe and Western Asia, and the C. mountain-system has no immediate connexion with any of the other great mountainsystems of Europe or of Asia, yet it is now at least common to regard the C. mountains as an Asiatic system. What are called the Caucasian districts are bounded on the N by the Kuban and the Terek, into the steppes of which the mountains gradually sink; and on the S by the Black sea, the Rioni, and the Kur. The perimeter of this district presents, in general outline, the figure of an obliqueangled parallelogram, having its largest or inland sides inclined towards the SW .- The C. chain offers thirteen principal basins to the notice of the physical geographer. Of these, 7 belong to the N, and 6 to the S side. But without entering on these details, we shall proceed to describe this chain under three great divisions: viz. the Western, Central, and Eastern C. Western C.] The W portion of the chain may be considered as extending from the Black sea to the upper part of the courses of the Rioni and the Kuban: its E front or extremity being the lofty Elburz, which, according to the observations of M. Vichneoski, rises to the height of 2,783 toises, or 17,832 ft., above the level of the sea. The W extremity of this section is called by the Russians Tchernigori or 'Black mountains;' by the Circassians, Kushaa; from the circumstance of its being densely covered with foliage, and to distinguish it from the more elevated range towards the W, which is covered Fart of the line of the Russian Levelling Survey between the Black and Caspian Seas, for the purpose to ascertain the depression of the latter, made by Russ, Sawitzeh and Subler in 1836 and 1837.
