It is bounded on the NW by the sea of Azof; on the N by the territory of the Don Cossacks and the prov. of Astrakhan; the river Kugoi- Jeia and Lake Bolchoillmen separating it from the former; the Manytch, and the Kuma, separating it from the latter; on the E it has the gulf of Kuma in the Caspian sea; on the S, the prov. of Georgia, from which it is separated by the Terek; and by the watershed of the rivers flowing N to the Kuban, and S to the Rioni and the Black sea. It is comprised between the parallels of 42° 55' and 46° 40' N; and the meridians of 37° and 47° E. Measured from the mouth of the Terek to that of the Kuban, along a line running ESE and WNW, its length is nearly 600 m.; along the meridian of 42°, which nearly divides it into two equal portions, its breadth is 250 m. By Guibert its area is estimated at 10,575,000 hectares; by Arssenjew, at 94,707 square versts, or 41,556 English sq. m. This last admeasurement does not include the Tchernomorsk Cossack territories. In the article Russia in the Penny Cyclopædia the area of Caucasia is estimated at 40,000 sq. m.; and in the table given in Macgregor's Commercial Tariff of Russia, at 375,000 sq. m.! while the pop. is estimated in the former at 150,000; and in the latter at only 110,000. Such are the discrepancies existing on the subject of the extent of the Russian territories, and their pop., chiefly in consequence of the vague application of names. Thus, too, while Guibert states the pop. of C. in 1835 at 125,000, the Almanach de Gotha, on the authority of M. de Keppen, estimates the pop. at 402,800. Adopting Arssenjew's estimate of the superficies, and M. de Koppen's estimate of the pop., the density of the pop. seems to be about 10 to the sq. m., while that for all Russia is about 20. The N and W parts of this prov. generally present a level surface, being in fact prolongations of the great Astrakhan steppes. The S part is covered with a portion of the great Cancasian chain described in the next article. With the exception of its frontier-rivers, already noticed, it has few streams rising in the interior; but its lakes and salt-marshes are numerous. The climate is hot, and in many places insalubrious. Wood is rare, except on the outlying ridges of the Caucasus; and the only spots of highly fertile soil are found along the banks of the rivers, and produce rice, maize, barley, wheat, millet, sesame, tobacco, cotton, lint, silk, and wine. On the rich and extensive steppe-pastures immense herds of cattle, goats, and horses are reared. In 1830, the number of the pop. engaged in agriculture was returned at 103,884. The number of sheep held by them was 2,000,000. The chief articles of export are salt, cattle, horses, hides, caviare, and brandy.—The prov. is administered by a military and a civil governor, both of whom are under the superintendence of the governor-general of the Caucasian provinces. The civil pop. comprises about one-half of the inhabitants; about one-fourth is composed of the Cossacks of the military line along the Caucasus and the Cossacks of the Black sea; and the remainder are nomade Calmucks, Turkomans, and Nogay Tartars. Exclusive of the Cossacks of the Black sea, or the Tchernomorsk Cossacks, whose cap. is Ekaterinodar, the prov. is divided into the 4 districts of Stavropol, Georgiefsk, Kizliar, and Mozdok. From Kislar, at the mouth of the Terek, to Mozdok, and thence to Georgiefsk, there extends a line of forts and fortified posts which joins the Kuban at Batal-Pachinskoi, 65 m. to the W of Georgiefsk, and runs down that river to Labinskaia. 120 m. WNW of Stavropol. The defence of this line is intrusted to the Cossacks of the Line, as they are II. * called. From Labinskaia to the mouth of the Kuban, the defence is intrusted to the Cossacks of the Black sea. All the mountain-territory lying to the SW of this line, with the exception of a few fortresses on the coast of the Black sea, is still in the hands of the Circassian tribes. From Mozdok, or rather from Ekaterinograd higher up the Terek, a great military line of road is carried up the valley of the Terek through Kabardah, and across the Kazbek, by the pass of Dariel, to Teflis. The main road intersecting the prov. runs from Kislar, up the Terek, to Mozdok; thence to Georgiefsk; and thence to Stavropol, where it parts into two branches, one running NNW to Nakshivan, and the other W, along the N bank of the Kuban to the straits of Jenikaleh. Another steppe-road leads directly N from Mozdok, across the Kuma and the Manytch, into the prov. of Astrakhan. To effect and maintain some amount of control over the Circassian tribes occupying the Western Caucasus, it was proposed by Paskiewitch to carry a line of occupation from Ghelendik on the Black sea to the lower part of the Kuban; and another line from a point farther to the E, on the Abasian coast, over the Caucasus, by the Col-de-Jumatan, to the mineral springs in the valleys of the Elburz; but both these designs have been utterly baffled by the intrepidity of the Circassians, who repulsed the Russian general Williamoff, and captured or destroyed the new forts he had established between Olginsky on the Kuban and Pshad on the Black sea. The strength of the Russian army in the C. varies much from year to year with the rate of mortality and the arrival of reinforcements. In 1843, it amounted to 117,000 men, a number by no means disproportionate to the extent of its line of operations. This force it is reported suffers dreadfully from fever, which in some years carries off a sixth of the whole army.

CAU'CASUS, an extensive mountain-system extending, like an immense wall, across the isthmus between the Black sea and the Caspian, or from the mouth of the Kuban in 44° 50' N lat. and 37° 10' E long., on the NW, to Cape Apcheron on the Caspian, in 40° 21' N lat. and 49° 40' E long., on the SE. Although the isthmus of the C. forms a kind of connecting link betwixt Europe and Western Asia, and the C. mountain-system has no immediate connexion with any of the other great mountainsystems of Europe or of Asia, yet it is now at least common to regard the C. mountains as an Asiatic system. What are called the Caucasian districts are bounded on the N by the Kuban and the Terek, into the steppes of which the mountains gradually sink; and on the S by the Black sea, the Rioni, and the Kur. The perimeter of this district presents, in general outline, the figure of an obliqueangled parallelogram, having its largest or inland sides inclined towards the SW .- The C. chain offers thirteen principal basins to the notice of the physical geographer. Of these, 7 belong to the N, and 6 to the S side. But without entering on these details, we shall proceed to describe this chain under three great divisions: viz. the Western, Central, and Eastern C. Western C.] The W portion of the chain may be considered as extending from the Black sea to the upper part of the courses of the Rioni and the Kuban: its E front or extremity being the lofty Elburz, which, according to the observations of M. Vichneoski, rises to the height of 2,783 toises, or 17,832 ft., above the level of the sea. The W extremity of this section is called by the Russians Tchernigori or 'Black mountains;' by the Circassians, Kushaa; from the circumstance of its being densely covered with foliage, and to distinguish it from the more elevated range towards the W, which is covered with perpetual snow. These Black mountains do not present a continuous line; but are rent and broken into chasms and ridges stretching NW and SW, and forming gorges and defiles impracticable to any army. [Spenser.] From the N or Georgian side of this part of the chain, the following rivers descend: viz. the Atakum, the Kara-Kuban, the Shakwassa, the Laba, the Urup, the Great and the Little Zelutchuk or Indjik, the Koiden, and the Teberde. From the S or Great Abasian and Mingrelian side, the following small streams flow: viz. the Subachi, the Kapoeti, the Mutsi, the Zupi, the Alatso, the Sokhumi, the Khodori, the Marmari, the Mokvi, the Egrisi, the Dadi, and the Eguri. The principal defiles of the Western C. occur at the sources of the Shakwassa, the Great Laba, and the Teberde. The first, or the most western, establishes a communication betwixt the valley of Shakwassa and the Abasian port of Soukhoum-kaleh. The second leads to the villages of Mokvi and Khodori. The W branch of the third leads, through the territory of the Suanes, to Bedia on the Egrisi. The E branch of this pass descends to the sources of the Tschenis-tzquali, from which it leads into Imeritia and Mingrelia. From the Elburz and along the r. bank of the Kuban, a branch runs N from this chain, presenting various isolated summits, such as the Soistun, and those of the Temnoi-less. Central C.] The second part of the principal chain commences at the foot of Mount Elburz, under the name of the Snowy mountains, and runs E to a culminating point from which the Sundsha and the Aksai flow toward the N, the Khaeseruk and Quozloukhi to the E, and the Alazan and Jora to the S. Here the chain is remarkably rugged, and surmounted with glaciers. At the sources of the Jinaghi-don this chain changes its direction, and runs SSE, under the Georgian name of Kedela, or 'the Wall,' to the sources of the Ratchis-tzquali and the Kuirili. From this latter point it takes an E direction, and runs to join the gigantic Khokhi, which rises above the sources of the Terek. This third section, or subdivision, of the central chain is called Brouts-sabdzeli or Sekara. From the Khokhi it runs SE to the sources of the Aragvi, where it forms the Cross mountain. It then bends again towards the E, separates the tributary waters of the Terek and Sundsha from those of the Aragvi and Alazan, and reaches its ultimate point at the head of the valleys inhabited by the Meesti, the Pharsmani, and the Thoucheti. The regions situated to the N of the Central C. are: Little Abasia, Ossetia, the territory of the Mitsdjegi, and the two Kabardahs. On the opposite side are Imeritia, and Kartli or Kartalinia. The rivers which flow from the N side of Central C. are: the Kuma, the Podkumka, the Malka, the Bakzan, the Tcheghem, the Tcherek, the Uruk, the Arredon, the Fiag, the Tundsha, and the Aksai. From the S side descend the Rioni, the Tskhenistzquali and Kuirili, the Didi-liakhvi, the Khasani, and the Aragvi. To this portion of the chain belong: the Kachka-tau, the Lagat, the Mqinvari, elevated 15,400 feet, the Kuro, the Ouloumba, the Asmis-mtha, and the Lordsobani summits,—There are 7 passes leading through this part of the chain, the two principal of which run along the tributary torrents of the Uruk, from the Imeritian province of Ratcha, into the country of the Dongars, and thence, through the valleys of the Rioni and Bokvi, into Circassia. The Portæ Caucasiæ of the ancients is the defile of Dariel, which leads through this chain from Mozdok to Tiflis. It is a narrow valley of four days' journey, through which, according to Strabo, the river Aragos, now called Aragvi, flows. Pliny admirably describes it as an enormous work of Nature, which has here cut out a long opening among the rocks which an iron-gate would be almost sufficient to close. By this passage, according to Priscus, the barbarous Medes, Sarmatians, and other tribes, threatened both the Roman and the Persian empire. The Russians keep a strong garrison at Vladi-Kaukaz, the key to this defile, a fortress founded by Potemkin. The chief duty of this garrison is to escort the post to and from Petersburg, across the plain of the Kabardah, to Ekaterinograd on the Malka river, a distance of about 70 m., beyond which an escort is no longer necessary. Eastern C.] The Eastern C. extends to the peninsula of Apcheron, its general direction being from NW to SE. The W portion of it, as far as Gattunkul, on the S bank of the Samur, is generally regarded as forming a part of the Snowy mountains, but it is much less elevated. From this point a high chain of glaciers called Shah-dagh or Shat-dag run towards the Kuba, on the western side of which river we encounter Mount Shalburz or Shah-Alburz. The principal summits which occur to the E of Shalburz are: the Salavat - dagh, the Baha - dagh, the Kaler-dagh, and the Belira-dagh, -all granitic summits. To the W of the latter summit, the elevation of the chain is from 1,666 toises, or 10,674 ft., to 2,000 toises or 12,816 ft. Towards the E they gradually decline until they reach the peninsula of Apcheron where they are of trifling elevation. In the upper basin of the Samur or Quozloukhi, and in that of the Koizu and Atala, are the countries of the Kazi - Kumuks, and Avares, and the republic of Akusha. The other northern regions bathed by the Caspian are comprehended under the general name of Daghistan. The united basins of the Alazan and Yori, in the opposite quarter, form the district of Kakhetia. The tributaries of the Kur also flow on this side through Western Shirvan. The passes through the Eastern Caucasus are as yet but imperfectly known to European geographers. Geology and scenery.] The summits and central ridge of C. are granitic. On each side the granite has schistose mountains joining it; and these are succeeded by calcareous mountains which appear to occupy most space on the S side, where the chain runs out into a greater number of branches. On the N side, the base of the calcareous and schistose mountains is covered by vast sandy downs or plains. "The sloping plains formed by the C. mountains measured, even from their greatest elevations, from N to S, are not of equal length with those extending in the contrary direction from S to N. The southern slope is much longer than the northern; that is, from the highest chain to the branch of the limestone range. The length of the N slope is about 25 versts; that of the southern near 50, reckoning from Gelalethi to Anamori. The southern flank is of course less steep, and the descent more gradual; so that few pointed or pyramidal peaks are to be seen there; and, for the same reason also, it is not so bare, but is almost everywhere clothed with trees and herbage." [Klaproth.] The following is M. Klaproth's description of the scenery in the principal chain or the Alps of the C:—"At the foot of the snowy summits are found habitations which the owners have been tempted to construct in that situation by a few acres of ground susceptible of cultivation. In the valleys which separate these snowy mountains, glaciers are seen, which seem to recline upon blocks of ice and rock. These valleys are closed in at their superior extremities, by huge pieces of ice interlaid like the strata of rocks, seeming to owe their origin to icewater descending from the summits and congealed anew. The frozen masses are supported by arcades of ice, over which torrents are heard rushing with fearful noise by the traveller journeying beneath these vaults. Descending from the glaciers, we encounter fields of snow spread out on beds of ice. Granite and schistose, intersected frequently by basalts, are seen contiguous to other rocks of the black tabulary schistus, and, rising in sharp and naked peaks separated by deep ravines, ascend to the region of snow and perpetual ice. Torrents rush through these ravines, on the banks of which are found a few pines, junipers, and other trees peculiar to the frozen zone." M. Du Bois has discovered several epochs of elevation in the region of the C. The oldest of these is posterior to the Jura formation. Granitic masses have pierced the thick mass of black schist, and have dislocated it, heaving aside the strata of Jura limestone which were superimposed, and bursting the crust of the earth, these masses have raised from the depths of the ocean the first rudiments of what might be called the island of the C., and which was elevated several thousand feet above the level of the surrounding sea. An epoch of repose succeeded to this first cataclysm, and sedimentary deposits followed the first uprising. During this period of repose, the lower schist of the chalk and the green sand were calmly deposited; and each of these stages of deposit produced a formation of many thousand feet in thickness. The conclusion of the epoch of the green sand appears to have been marked by a new upraising, viz. that of the Akhalziké chain, the axis of which approximates in its direction to between E and W, nearly corresponding to that of the elevation of the sandstone and the marl in the Carpathian chain. The principal agent, in this new revolution, was melaphyre, or pyroxenous porphyry. It has cleft the chain in the greater part of its length, and has broken forth by this gap, turning aside on either side the two slopes of the chalk, under an angle of 30°, more or less, not unlike the roof of a house. After this second upraising, there existed, according to M. Du Bois, between the island already referred to and the Akhalziké range, a frith or sea, into which was deposited true chalk, along with all its characteristic fossils, and to which still corresponds that long depression which, under the name of Colchis and Georgia, skirts the S acclivity of the C. To the S of this depression, the traveller—continues M. Du Bois-finds himself in a labyrinth of volcanic amphitheatres, analogous to those which are seen upon the surface of the moon, and which here, crowding upon each other, fill the whole space between the Caspian and the Black sea. In traversing the peaks of Ketedagh and Kiskala, the volcanic amphitheatre of Lake Sevang [Sivan] is seen, elevated 5,000 ft. above the level of the ocean. To the SW of Lake Sevang, as if to proclaim in express terms what now is and what formerly was, you pass from a vast amphitheatre filled with an immense mass of water, to the vast central amphitheatre of Armenia now emptied of its fluid contents. The Kiotangdagh, the Agmangan, the Naltapa, and many other craters and volcanic cones, separate these two amphitheatres; whilst the Great Ararat, with an absolute height of 16,254 feet (French), the Little Ararat, whose height is 12,162 ft., the Sinak, and the Takhaltou to the S, and the Alaghez, 12,000 ft. high, to the NW, finish with their imposing cones the rest of that superb garland of extinct volcanoes whose agency has assisted in filling the basin of central Armenia or Ararad. Over all its circumference, nothing is to be seen but black and gray lava currents, with pumice or obsidian, along with scoriæ and basalt or trass, intermixed with porphyries and melaphyres. In passing from the banks of the Araxes to those of the Kur, you encounter the vol- origin has been argued for in the case of the Georcanic amphitheatre of the Upper Kur or Akhalziké. Over a vast space, of which Kertris perhaps is the centre, you find nothing but pyroxenic lava, along with cones of ashes, beds of scoriæ and lapillæ. But all these fragments of basins and volcanic uprisings are but feeble preludes to the last great effect, which was by far the most extensive; for it has certainly upraised the C. to a much greater height than it previously possessed, and elevated it to the height it now actually reaches. It was likewise the means of laying bare and dry all those arms of the sea, so to speak, which surrounded it; in other words, the regions of Colchis, Georgia, Daghistan, and all the vast steppes which so extensively surround the Black sea and the Sea of Azof, and which cover the Crimea. Not only did it form a volcanic outburst on the S of the C., but it gave birth to many volcanic chimneys in the very heart of the chain; the chief of these vents of eruption are the Elburz, the Passeinta, Kasbek, the Red mountains, &c. [Edin. New Phil. Journ., Oct. 1807.] Productions.] The mountain-range we are now describing presents every climate of Europe, and every kind of soil; its productions therefore are highly varied. "In the centre," says Malte Brun, "we have eternal ice and barren rocks, inhabited by bears, wolves, and jackals. Here also we find the chaus, an animal of the genus Felis; the wild goat of the C., Caprica Caucasica, which delights in the rugged summits of the schistose mountains; the chamois, which prefers the lower calcareous mountains; hares, weasels, polecats, ermines, argalis, and an infinite number of birds of prey and of passage. To the N are hills fertile in corn, and rich pastures whereon the fine Circassian horses are reared. Farther on occur sandy plains, covered with large plants, but mixed with low grounds of a more clayey soil. To the S you find magnificent valleys and plains, enjoying a more salubrious climate, and displaying all the luxuriance of an Asiatic vegetation. Wherever the declivity inclines towards the W, the E, or the S, cedars, cypresses, savins, red junipers, beech-trees, and oaks, clothe the sides of the mountains. The almond, the peach, and the fig, grow in abundance in the warmer valleys under the shelter of the rocks; and the quince, the wild apricot, the willow-leaved peartree, and the vine, abound in the thickets and woods, and on the borders of the forests. The date-tree, the jujube, and Christ's thorn, are indigenous in this country, and prove the mildness of the temperature. The low marshy grounds are adorned with very fine plants, such as the Rhododendron Ponticum, and the Azalea Pontica. The cultivated and wild olive-trees, the oriental plane, together with the male and female laurels, embellish the coasts of the Caspian sea. The high valleys are perfumed by the syringa, the jessamine, several species of lilies, and the Caucasian rose." In the Western C. the oak, the beech, and the linden, attain a growth seldom equalled in Europe; while the box and the juniper flourish with a luxuriance incredible to a European. In addition to these, the mulberry, chestnut, pomegranate, and vine, grow there in profusion. C. Tribes.] The mountaineers of the C. are of various origin; and their tribes are to this day comparatively distinct and isolated, unlike each other, and unlike the rest of the world. It is said that upwards of 70 dialects have been distinguished amongst these tribes; and that, though these are not all radically distinct from each other, yet they form several unconnected groups, bearing no resemblance to the conterminous Turkish, Persian, Russian, or Armenian languages. Bopp has endeavoured to prove them to be Indo-European. An Egyptian gian dialects, as the representative of the old Colchian. Klaproth has succeeded in pointing out some affinities amongst them with the Siberian dialects. Our own eminent philologist Dr. Latham contends that their nearest affinities are with the monosyllabic languages, of which those of Tibet are the nearest in geographical position. Some are the remains of Asiatic hordes which, in the great migrations, passed and repassed these mountains; but the greater numbers are indigenous and primitive tribes. The Georgians and the Ossetes are the new inhabitants of this territory. The former came from the Pampaki valleys between the Caucasus and Ararat. The Ossetes are said to be the descendants of a Scythian colony which was established here about 7 centuries before the Christian era. The Basianes, a Turkish tribe from Madjari and the banks of the Kuma, at present inhabit the upper valleys of the Malka, the Tcheghem, and the Tcherek. They are supposed to have quitted their primitive abodes in the 8th cent. before the Christian era. The primitive Caucasians are: 1st, the Eastern Caucasians or Lesghis; 2d, the Mizdsheges in the centre; and 3d, the Western Caucasians, or Circassians and Abasses. In the following list, we have distinguished the nations and countries included in the Russian territories by an r, and the states which yet retain their independence by an i:- Number of houses. T. . . Samakhi, The country of the Khevsuri, Pshavi, and Touchi, 2,300 T. The Lesghis of Avar, Andi, &c., . Total, . Reckoning 5 individuals to a house, the total pop. of the C. territories will thus amount to 2,353,820 souls. Of this number about one-fourth are free; the rest are subjects of the czar. M. Hommaire de Hell has furnished us with the following very distinct outline of the respective positions of the independent tribes of the C. and their nominal rulers, viewed in relation to the topographical features of the district. "The chain of the C.," he remarks, "exhibits a peculiar conformation, altogether different from that of any of the European chains. The Alps, the Pyrenees, and the Carpathians, are accessible only from their valleys; and in these the inhabitants of the country find their subsistence, and agriculture develops its resources. The contrary is the case in the C. From the fortress of Anapa on the Black sea, all along to the Caspian, the N slope presents only immense inclined plains, rising in terraces to a height of 3,000 or 4.000 yards above sea-level. These plains, rent in all directions by deep and narrow valleys and on their loftiest heights rich pastures, where the inhabitants, secure from all attack, find fresh grass for their cattle in the sultriest days of summer. The valleys, on the other hand, are frightful abysses, the steep sides of which are clothed with brambles, while the bottoms are filled with rapid torrents foaming over beds of rocks and stones. Such is the singular spectacle generally presented by the N slope of the C. This brief description may give an idea of the difficulties to be encountered by an invading army. Obliged to occupy the heights, it is incessantly checked in its march by impassable ravines, which do not allow of the employment of cavalry, and for the most part prevent the passage of artillery. The ordinary tactics of the mountaineers is to fall back before the enemy, until the nature of the ground or the want of supplies obliges the latter to begin a retrograde movement. Then it is that they attack the invaders, and, entrenched in their forests behind impregnable rocks, they inflict the most terrible carnage on their foes with little danger to themselves. On the S the character of the C. chain is different. From Anapa to Gagra, along the shores of the Black sea, we observe a secondary chain composed of schistose mountains, seldom exceeding 1,000 yards in height. But the nature of their soil, and of their rocks, would be enough to render them almost impracticable for European armies, even were they not covered with impenetrable forests. The inhabitants of this region,—who are called Tcherkesses or Circassians, by the Russians,-are entirely indemense crags that completely cut off all approach to the central part of the C. This region, so feebly forced to submit to Russia. To the N, and on the N slope, westward of the military road from Mozdok some of them ruled by a sort of feudal system, others constituted into little republics. Those on the W, dependent on Circassia and Abadza, are in conand the tribes of the Great Kabarda, own the sove- [Suanes], an unsubdued people; and near them, occupying both sides of the pass of Dariel, are the Ingushes and Ossetians, exceptional tribes, essentially different from the aboriginal peoples. Finally, we have to the E of the great Tiflis coast, near the Terek, Little Kabardah and the country of the Kumiks, both for the present subjugated; and then those indomitable tribes, the Lesghis and Tchetchenzes, of whom Shamihl is the Abd-el-Kader, and who extend over the two slopes of the C. to the vicinity of the Caspian. In reality, the Kuban and the Terek rivers, which rise in the central chain, and fall, the one into the Black sea, the other into the Caspian, may be considered as the northern political limits of independent C. It is along those two rivers that Russia has formed an armed line, defended by Cossacks and detachments from the regular army. The Russians have indeed penetrated those N frontiers at sundry points, and have planted some forts within the country of the Lesghis and Tchetchenzes; but these lonely posts, in which a few unhappy garrisons are surrounded on all sides, and generally without vertical clefts, often form real steppes, and present | a chance of escape, cannot be regarded as a real occupation of the soil on which they stand. They are in fact only so many piquets, whose business is only to watch more closely the movements of the mountaineers. In the S, from Anapa to Gagra, along the Black sea, the imperial possessions are limited to a few detached forts, completely isolated, and deprived of all means of communication by land. A rigorous blockade has been established on this coast; but the Circassians, as intrepid in their frail barks as among their mountains, often pass by night through the Russian line of vessels, and reach Trebisond and Constantinople. Elsewhere, from Mingrelia to the Caspian, the frontiers are less precisely defined, and generally run parallel with the great chain of the Caucasus. Thus limited, the C., including the territory occupied by the subject tribes, presents a surface of scarcely 5,000 leagues; and it is in this narrow region that a virgin and chivalric nation, amounting at most to 2,000,000 of souls, proudly upholds its independence against the might of the Russian empire, and has for twenty [thirty] years sustained one of the most obstinate struggles known in modern history." Name.] The name Caucasus is very ancient; but much diversity of opinion prevails regarding its origin. The most ancient etymology is that supplied by Pliny, who derives it from a Scythian word, graucasus, signifying 'whitened by snow.' "As, however," says Klaproth, "this etymology is not confirmed by any known language, and it is extremely improbable that the whole family of words to which it belongs should have been lost, it seems to carry little weight, and to be equally worthless with many others set up by the ancients. Kaukas, which is a foreign term in these mountain-districts, may perhaps come from the Persian appellation Koh-ckaf, which signifies 'the mountains of Ckaf.' The more ancient form of this latter word was probably Ckafsp or Ckassp, with the termination assp, which was common in the Median dialects. From this ancient form of ckaf, the Caspian sea and the nation of the Caspians probably received their name; for, according to the testimony of Eratosthenes, as quoted by Strabo, the people inhabiting the Caucasus called it 'the Caspian mountain' [Karriov ogos]. In Moses of Chorene it is called Kowkass and Kaukass; and in the History of Georgia, compiled by the direction of King Wachtang V., from the archives of the convents of Mzchetha and Gelathi, the most ancient boundaries of this country are thus described: - On the E it has the Gurganian sea [Gurganissa], now called the sea of Gilan; on the W the Pontic, otherwise the Black sea; on the S the Orethian mountains [Orethissa], situated in the country of the Kurds [Khurthia] towards Media; and on the N the Kawkasian mountains [Khawk'assia], which are called by the Persians Jalbus.' In the epitome of the history of the country, written by the Georgian prince Davith, and printed at Tiflis in 1798, the C. is styled, from ancient authorities, Kawk'ass. 'The country belonging to him [i. e. to Thargamoss] was bounded on the E by the Gurganian sea [that is, the Caspian]; on the W by the Black sea [the Pontus]; on the S by the Aressian mountains [those of Kurdistan]; and on the N by the K'awk'assian.' All this," Klaproth continues, "sufficiently proves the autiquity of the name Caucasus among the neighbouring nations; nevertheless, at present it is but little used by the Asiatics, who commonly call this mountain by the Tartar name of Jalbus, that is, 'Ice-mane.' In Tartar the appellation is properly Jalbus-thaglar; but among the Nogays I have likewise heard it pronounced Jildis-thaglar, in which case it signifies 'the Mountains of the Stars.' By the Turks the C. is named Ckaf Thagi, or 'Mountains of Ckaf.' The Georgians usually employ the Tartar term, and say Jalbusiss Mtha, i. e. 'Mount Jalbus.' The Armenians call it Jaibusi-ssar; but the name Kawkas also is retained by them." Historical notice.] In the imagination of the classical scholar this region is identified with the scene of Prometheus's ever-during punishment; to others the magnificent fictions of Arabian romance have clothed it with poetical mystery. Deucalion and Pyrrha, and the enterprising Argonauts, are associated with its classical history; and here Oriental fancy has placed the abode of the Preadamite monarchs, the retreat of the Dives, the Peris, and the Genii. Sesostris planted a colony of Egyptians at its foot, who founded Colchis. In the 7th cent. before the Christian era, the Milesians began to found settlements on the NE shores of the Black sea: the city of Tanais was built by them at the mouth of the Don, those of Phanagoria and Hermonassa on the Cimmerian Bosphorus, and Dioscurias in Mingrelia. The Scythians passed under these mountains on their way to the conquest of Upper Asia; Mithridates and Pompey left traces of their footsteps here; and Trajan extended his domination as far as this mountain-barrier. Long after the struggle of the Roman and Persian powers, the Arabs bore the crescent to the Caucasus. Then Jenghiz-Khan's moguls ravaged the countries to the foot of this barrier. Timur the Tartar's devastating hordes came next; and after them the Turkomans. Russia next seized upon CAU the Caucasian territories, but Nadir-Shah compelled them to relinquish this conquest. Escaping from Persian dominion, the Georgian princes threw themselves into the arms of Russia; and finally the treaty of Gulistan, in 1813, left Russia sole mistress of the Caucasian districts. Authorities.] When the empress Catherine issued commands that the whole empire of Russia should be surveyed and described by different members of the Academy of Sciences, the C. and Georgia were assigned to Professor Güldenstädt, whose Travels, though excellent in plan, were edited, Klaproth asserts, by a very incompetent party. After Güldenstädt, Reineggs visited the C. under the patronage of Count Kohari, and his Kaukasus was published in Germany in 1796, in 2 vols. 8vo. Reineggs was followed by Klaproth, under the patronage of Count Potocki, whose Travels in 1807 and 1808 were translated, and published in London in 1814 in a 4to vol. Our own traveller Dr. E. D. Clarke, while visiting Ekaterinedara, the cap. of the Tchernomorski Cossacks, dared not venture beyond protection of the guns of the fortress in his explorations of the Circassian side of the Kuban. M. Gamba, who travelled through the C. provinces in 1818 and 1819, and afterwards, while French consul at Tiflis, between the years 1820 and 1824, made different excursions in these provinces, published his Voyage dans la Russie Meridionale, in 2 vols. 8vo. In 1827, M. Klaproth published his Tableau hist. geog. ethnog. et polit. du C. in 8vo. Eichwald's Travels along the Caspian and on the C., a most important contribution to geographical science, appeared at Berlin in 1837-8 in 2 vols. 8vo. In 1836 Captain Spencer travelled, in the suite of the governorgeneral of South Russia, on the Western C.; and the results of his observations, published in 1838, in 2 vols. 8vo, are particularly valuable, as the statements of Güldenstädt, Reineggs, and Klaproth relative to Circassia had not been derived from personal observation. The latest publication on the C. is the Kaukasus, und das Land der Kosaken of Moritz Wagner, being an account of his travels on the C. and among the Cossacks in the years 1843 to 1846.

CAUCASUS (Indian), a name sometimes given to that portion of the great Himalaya mountain-system which is generally known as the Hindukush; but by some geographers confined to that portion of the chain which stretches from the upper valley of the Indus on the E, to the Kama on the W, or the section sometimes known as the Central Hindukush.
CAUCHOISE, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of the Seine-Inferieure, cant. and com. of Rouen. Pop. 3,000.
CAUDAN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Morbihan, cant. of Pont-Scorff. Pop. in 1841, 3,059. There is a navy dock-yard here.
CAUDAR, a small island of the Asiatic archipelago, in the Banda sea, in S lat. 5° 2', E long. 132° 10', 100 m. SE of Ceram.

CAUDEBEC, a canton, commune, town and port of France, in the dep. of the Seine-Inferieure, arrond. of Yvetot.—The cant. comprises 15 com. Pop. in 1831, 13,507; in 1841, 13,560.—The town is built in an amphitheatral form, at the foot of a woody hill, on the r. bank of the Seine, and at the junction of the river of the same name. Pop. in 1841, 2,616. The only object of architectural interest which it now contains is the parish-church, a handsome structure of the 15th cent. It possesses, however, considerable manufactories of cotton and woollen fabrics, cotton spinning-mills, tanneries, bleacheries, &c.; and has an active trade in grain, fruit, dried legumes, cider, and timber. Fairs are held three times a-year, and a grain-market weekly. The total movement of the port of C. in 1837 amounted to 19,742 tons. There is a light on Caudebecquet, 1,300 yards E of C. church.—This town, the ancient Lotum or Latomagus, was formerly the cap. of the Pays de Caux, and a place of considerable strength. It was ceded to the English in 1419; taken by the Protestants in 1562; and by the Leaguers in 1592. It has greatly declined in importance since the revocation of the edict of Nantes in 1685.

CAUDEBEC-LES-ELBEUF, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Seine-Inferieure, cant. of and 15 m. from Elbeuf, on the Oison. Pop. in 1831, 3,930; in 1841, 6,051. It possesses extensive manufactories of cloth, woollen spinning-mills, and dve-works.

CAUDEBRONDE, a commune of France, in the ~ +#27/12/01/24/04/4#24/5/24/04/04/04/04/04/04/04/04/04/04/04/04/04 dep. of Aude, cant. of Mas-Cabardes, 13 m. from Carcassonne. Pop. 567. It contains manufactories of cloth and other woollen fabrics, and a fullingmill.
CAUDECOSTE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Lot-et-Garonne, cant. of Astaffort, 9 m. SE of Agen. Pop. 1,186.
CAUDERAN, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Gironde, cant. and 2 m. W of Bordeaux. Pop. in 1841, 3,490. It contains the military hospital of Bordeaux.

CAUDETE, a town of Spain, in Murcia, in the prov. and 50 m. ESE of Albacete, partido and 16 m. SE. of Almansa, on the hill of Santa Ana. Pop. 6,500. It was formerly fortified, and contains a few Roman remains, and several churches and schools, 2 convents, an hospital, and an ancient episcopal palace. It has some manufactories of brandy.

CAUDIES-DE-SAINT-PAUL, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Pyrenees-Orientales, cant. of Saint-Paul-de-Fenouillet, 3 m. WNW of Perpignan. Pop. 1,327. It has an annual fair, the trade of which consists in wool, cattle, horses, mules, sheep, poultry, grain, cloth, &c.
CAUDROT, or CAUDEROT, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Gironde, cant. of Saint- Macaire, on the r. bank of the Garonne, 6 m. W of La Reole. Pop. 1,307.
CAUDRY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Nord, cant. of Clary, 10 m. from Cambrai. Pop. in 1841, 3,318. It has manufactories of tulle.

CAUGHNAWAGA, a village of Mohawk township, in Montgomery co., state of New York, U. S., on the N side of the Mohawk. Pop. in 1840, 200.— Also an Indian v. in Lower Canada, on the r. bank of the St. Lawrence, opposite to and about 1 m. distant from Lachine. It is proposed to execute a railroad from this point to join the Plattsburg and Montreal railroad, in the township of Mooer's, in Clinton co., in the state of New York, U.S., a distance of 29 m.
CAU'HABURI, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Para, which flows SSW, and unites with the Rio Negro 60 m. WNW of San-Antonio-de-Castenheiro.
CAUINARI, a river of New Grenada, in Ecuador, which unites with the Caqueta or Japura, on the confines of Brazil.
CAULDON, a parish of Staffordshire, 7 m. NW of Ashborne. Area 1,570 acres. Pop. in 1841, 326.
CAULDWELL, a hamlet in the p. of Stapenhill, Derbyshire, 4 m. NNE of Burton-upon-Trent. Pop. in 1841, 153.
CAULK, or CALKE, a parish of Derbyshire, 4 m. N of Ashby-de-la-Zouche. Area 880 ac. Pop. 55.
CAULNES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Cotes-du-Nord, cant. of Saint-Jouan-de-l'Isle, 15 m. SSW of Dinan. Pop. 1,897.

CAUMONT, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Calvados, arrond. of Bayeux. The cant. comprises 19 com. Pop. in 1831, 11,177; in 1841, 11,311. The town is finely situated on a hill 18 m. SSW of Bayeux. Pop. in 1836, 841; in 1846, 910. It has a considerable trade in horses, cattle, and poultry, the rearing of the latter of which forms an important branch of local industry. In the environs is an iron-mine. - Also a commune in the dep. of the Eure, cant. of Routot, near the l. bank of the Seine, 20 m. E of Pont-Audemer. Pop. 931. It contains extensive quarries of freestone, and iron-forges. - Also a commune and town in the dep. of the Lot-et- Garonne, cant. of Le Mas-d'Agénois, on an eminence on the l. bank of the Garonne, 4 m. S of Marmande. Pop. in 1836, 1,021; in 1846, 2,506.—Also a commune and small town in the dep. of Vaucluse, cant. of Cavaillon, on the r. bank of the Durance, 9 m. CAU ESE of Avignon. Pop. in 1841, 1,927. It is walled, and defended by an ancient castle.
CAUNDLE-BISHOP, a parish of Dorset, 5 m. SE of Sherborne. Area 2,050 acres. Pop. 365.
CAUNDLE-MARSH, a parish of Dorset, 4 m. SE of Sherborne. Area 440 acres. Pop. 77.
CAUNDLE-PURSE, a parish of Dorset, 41 m. ENE of Sherborne. Area 1,610 acres. Pop. 183.
CAUNDLE-STOURTON, a parish of Dorset, 6 m. E of Sherborne. Area 2,680 acres. Pop. 394.

CAUNE (LA), a mountain of France, in the dep. of Tarn. It detaches from the Cevennes on the confines of the dep. of Herault, 12 m. ESE of the town of the same name, and extends W about 60 m. between the basins of the Adour and Agout.—The town is situated on an affluent of the latter river, 20 m. ENE of Castres. Pop. 2,500. It possesses manufactories of hosiery, coarse calico, and dimity.

CAUNES (Les), a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Aude, cant. of Peyriac-Minervois, on a hill on the l. bank of the Argent-Double, 13 m. NE of Carcassonne. Pop. in 1836, 2,258; in 1846, 2,110. It contains a fine church, which belonged to the ancient Benedictine abbey suppressed at the revolution; and has extensive manufactories of cloth, distilleries of brandy, tanneries, a dye-work, and marble-works. The environs are celebrated for their marbles. This town was in the 4th cent. a village named Bufentis.
CAUNETTE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of Herault, cant. of Olonzac. Pop. 601. It contains extensive lignite mines, which produced in 1839, 47,586 quintals, valued at 78,488 francs.

CAUGMAHRY, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bengal, district and 40 m. NW of Dacca. CAUNPOOR, CAWNPORE, or KHANPUR, a rich and fertile district of Upper India, in the Doab, and chiefly between the Ganges and the Jumna rivers; bounded by Oude on the NE; by Futtehpur and Kalpec on the S; by Bundlecund on the SW; and by Etawa, Bilah, and Ferrukabad, on the NW. Area 2,650 sq. m. Its surface is level; and the soil is rich and highly cultivated. Its land-revenue in 1813-14 amounted to £334,700. After the battle of Buxar in 1801, this district was ceded to the British government by the vizier afterwards created king of Oude.—Its cap., of the same name, is situated on the W side of the Ganges, in N lat. 26° 30', E long. 80° 13', 625 m. from Calcutta, 38 m. WSW of Lucknow. It is built in a straggling manner, on a sandy plain broken occasionally into ravines. Its chief buildings are the military hospital, assembly-room, theatre, customhouse, and a Protestant church. The European houses are most of them large and roomy, standing in extensive 'compounds.' Several European merchants and shopkeepers reside here, and two or three indigo planters. As a military station, C. contests with Meerut the honour of being the most important in Upper Hindostan, for the necessity of maintaining an extra available force contiguous to the capital of Oude has led to the concentration of a large body of troops at C. The garrison usually consists of a European regiment of dragoons and one of native cavalry; several battalions of infantry, horse, and foot; a major-general in command, and the numerous staff attached to the head-quarters of a large district. The cantonments stretch 6 m. along the banks of the river. During the cold season, all the infantry corps usually encamp upon a wide plain in the vicinity of C., and the artillery likewise form a camp of their own. C. is not a very pleasant place of residence; the heat, drought, and dust, are excessive during the dry season; but the cold months are dry and bracing. Its principal manufactures are saddlery, harness, and gloves. *
CAUNTON, a parish of Nottinghamshire, 5 m. | NW of Newark, on a branch of the Trent. Area 3,130 acres. Pop. in 1841, 539.
CAUPENNE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Landes, cant. of Mugron. Pop. 1,009. Wine is grown in the locality.
CAUQUENES, a town of Chile, in the prov. of Maule, and on an affluent of the Maule, 110 m. NE of Concepcion.

CAUQUENES (BANOS - DE-), mineral baths of Chile, in the prov. and 70 m. SSE of Santiago, in a beautiful valley of the Andes, near the source of the Cachapoal. The springs are numerous, and differ extremely in temperature.
CAUSEWAY, a village in the p. of Killury, in co. Kerry, 8 m. W of Listowel. Pop. 527.
CAUSEWAY. See GIANTS' CAUSEWAY.
CAUSLUND, a parish and v. of Denmark, in Funen, SE of Mittelfahrt. Pop. 550.

CAUSSADE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Tarn-et-Garonne, arrond. of Montauban. The cant. comprises 10 com. Pop. in 1836, 14,158; in 1846, 13,699. The town, which is a pretty one, is 15 m. NE of Montauban, near the 1. bank of the Caude. Pop. in 1841, 4,292. It contains manufactories of woollen and linen fabrics, and of sugar from beet-root; and has a considerable trade in grain, flour, gin, poultry, cattle, dried fruit, truffles, and saffron. Fairs are held ten times a-year.
CAUSSOU, a village of France, in the dep. of Ariege, cant. of Cabanes, 19 m. SE of Foix. Pop. 449. The environs contain mines of silver, copper, lead, and iron.
CAUTARIO, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Matto-Grosso, which rises in the Serra-Parecis, runs SSW, and falls into the r. side of the Guapore, 80 m. E of the fort of Principe-da-Beira.

CAUTEN, or IMPERIAL, a river of Chile, which takes its rise in the Andes, to the W of the volcano of Chinal; runs W, and receives the Quepe on the 1.; unites with the Colpi and its tributaries; and after a total course of 210 m. falls into the Pacific 20 m. W of Imperial, in S lat. 38° 47′ 40″, W long. 73° 26'. Its embouchure is capable of receiving vessels of the largest size.—The town of C. is situated on the N bank, 40 m. E of Imperial.

CAUTERETS, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Hautes-Pyrenees, cant. and 9 m. S of Argeles, on a rapid mountain-stream, and in a valley, "a small triangular hollow" surrounded by lofty pineclad mountains, at an alt. of 2,900 ft. above sea-level. Pop. in 1841, 1,054. The valley contains eleven much-frequented mineral springs of a temp. from 31° to 40°. The v. always contains an indigenous pop. of about 600 or 800; and in the buildings appropriated to the reception of visitors nearly 1,000 persons can be accommodated. C. is better sheltered than Barréges, and is not subject to its fogs; while it is more open than the locality of Eaux-Chaudes.
CAUVERY. See CAVERI.

CAUX, formerly a district of France, in the prov. of Normandy, now comprised in the dep. of the Seine-Inferieure. It contained Caudebec the earlier, and Dieppe the later cap., Yvetot, Eu, Arques, Le Havre, Fécamp, Montivilliers, and Harfleur. It was the Caletes of Cæsar, of which Juliobona (Lillebonne) was the cap.—Also a commune in the dep. of Herault, cant. of Pezenas, 15 m. NE of Beziers. Pop. in 1841, 1,677.

CAVA, a town of Naples, cap. of a circondario, in the prov. of Principato-Citra, district and 4 m. NW of Salerno, and 27 m. ESE of Naples. Pop. 13,095. It contains a cathedral, 3 churches, a theological seminary, a monastery, a military hospital, and an alms-house; and possesses extensive manu- CAV factories of silk, cotton, and linen fabrics.—At the distance of 12 m. from this town is the celebrated Benedictine abbey of Santa-Trinita-de-Cava. The library and archives of this abbey-now transferred to Naples-were the richest in the kingdom.-Also a village of Piedmont, in the prov. of Lomellina, 21 m. SE of Mortara. Pop. 1,200.—Also a town of the States-of-the-Church, in the comarca and 25 m. ESE of Rome, and 5 m. SSE of Palestrina.
CAVA, a small island of Scotland, in the Orkney group, 11 m. SE of Houton-head. Pop. 23. It is a little above 3 m. in circuit.
CAVACURTA, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. of Lodi and Crema, 15 m. SE of Lodi. Pop. 1,450.

CAVADO, or CABADO, a river of Portugal, which takes its rise in the Serra-de-Gerez, in the prov. of Tras-os-Montes; passes Montalegre; runs S, then enters the prov. of Entre-Douro-e-Minho and directs its course WSW, receiving the Homem on the r., and passing Barcellos and Esposendo; and after a total course of 60 m., of which 6 only are navigable, falls into the Atlantic a little below the latter town.
CAVAGLIA, a town of Piedmont, in the prov. and 15 m. S of Biella. Pop. 2,600.

CAVAILLON, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Vaucluse, arrond. of Avignon. The cant. comprises 6 com. Pop. in 1836, 12,729; in 1846, 13,359. The town is situated at the foot of a hill, on the r. bank of the Durance, near the confluence of the Coulon, 20 m. ESE of Avignon. Pop. in 1836, 7,165; in 1846, 7,327. Its streets are narrow and dirty, but it contains a handsome town-house and possesses numerous silk-spinningmills, tanneries, a copper-work, and a manufactory of vermicelli. The trade, which is very active, consists in raw silk, dried fruit, olives, nuts, &c. Fairs are held three times a-year. The environs contain extensive quarries of freestone, and produce large quantities of fruit, including olives and melons, madder and silk. C., the Cabellio of the Romans, is of great antiquity, and was in the Roman era a town of considerable importance. It still exhibits numerous antiquities, of which the most remarkable are the ruins of a triumphal arch supposed to have belonged to the Augustan age.
CAVAILLON, a town in the island of St. Domingo, 9 m. W of Saint-Louis, and 99 m. WSW of Port-au-Prince.
CAVAIVA, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Matto-Grosso, which falls into the Tapajos, 10 m. NE of the confluence of the Rio-dos-Arinos.
CAVAJONE, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. and 14 m. NW of Verona, district and 5 m. S of Caprino. Pop. 1,500. It has an annual fair.
CAVAK, a town of Turkey in Europe, in the prov. of Romelia, sanjak and 22 m. NE of Gallipoli, on the l. bank and near the entrance of a river of the same name into the gulf of Saros.
CAVALAINE, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Var, cant. of Saint-Tropez, com. of Gassin, on the Mediterranean. Pop. 50. It has a small port.

CAVALCANTE, a town and parish of Brazil, cap. of a comarca of the same name, in the prov. of Goyaz, to the W of the Paranan, 50 m. SW of the mountain of Morro-do-Chapeo, about the same distance E of San-Felis and 245 m. NNE of Goyaz, in N lat. 13° 30'. Pop. 4,000. It contains 2 churches and a school. Agriculture, and the rearing of cattle for sale in the maritime provinces, form the chief employments of the inhabitants. The district is watered by numerous affluents of the Tocantins. It formerly contained valuable gold mines.
CAVALCASELLE, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. and 14 m. W of Verona, district of Bardolino, near the SE extremity of Lake Garda. A fair is held here once a-year.
CAVALERIE (LA), a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Aveyron, cant. of Nant, 9 m. SSE of Millau. Pop. 1,749.
CAVALER-MAGGIORE, a town of Piedmont, in the prov. and 21 m. NNE of Coni, on the r. bank of the Maira, and 4 m. N of Savigliano. Pop. 5,200. It contains 2 churches and 4 convents.

CAVALESE, a town of Austria, in Tyrol, in the gov. of Innspruck, circle and 20 m. NE of Trent, near the r. bank of the Avisio and at the foot of the Schwarzhorn. It contains a convent, and has a considerable trade in timber. Fairs are held three times a-year.

CAVALIE'RE (CAPE), the Sarpedon promontorium of the ancients, a broad headland or peninsula on the S coast of Asia Minor, in the pash. of Itchili, sanjak and 21 m. SW of Salevke, in N lat. 36° 1′ 50", and E long. 33° 42′ 40", united to the continent by a low narrow isthmus, of which all the accessible parts are fortified. It consists of white marble, and rises to the height of 600 or 700 ft.—A little to the NE is an islet of the same name, 600 fath. long, and 100 fath. broad.—Also a promontory of the SW coast of Anatolia, in the sanjak of Mentesha, opposite the island and on the E side of the gulf of Simi.

CAVALLA, a town of Turkey, in Romelia, 90 m. NE of Salonica, celebrated as having been the birthplace of Mahomet Ali, the late ruler of Egypt, who was born here in 1769. The town is situated on a piece of land projecting into the sea.
CAVALLINHOS (Rio-dos), a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Santa-Catharina, an affluent of the channel which separates the island of San-Francisco from the continent. It is navigable for 8 m.

CAVALLO (CAPE), a headland of Naples, on the NE coast of the prov. of Otranto, 5 m. E of Brindisi, in N lat. 40° 38'.—Also a broad elbow of land on the coast of Greece, in N lat. 38°, E long. 24° 2', forming the S coast of the bay of Marathon.
CAVALLO, an island in the straits of Bonifacio, near the SE extremity of the island of Corsica, in N lat. 41° 23′, and E long. 9° 16′. It contains traces of ancient granite quarries.
CAVALLOS, a mountain of Brazil, in the prov. of Santa-Catharina, which is crossed by the Strada imperial or imperial road.
CAVALLOS ISLANDS, a group of islands near the W coast of Portugal, prov. of Entre-Douro-e- Minho, to the SW of the embouchure of the Cavado, in N lat. 41° 30'.
CAVALLOS (Rio Dos), a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Rio-Grande-do-Norte. It forms the principal embouchure of the Piranhas, and is navigable for the largest vessels.

CAVALLY, a town of Upper Guinea, on the Grain coast, at the mouth of the river of the same name, 25 m. ENE of Cape Palmas. The pop. has been stated to amount to 10,000. The environs are fertile and afford excellent pasturage. The river C. forms to some extent the line of separation between tho Grain and Ivory coasts, and is navigable to some distance for small vessels. It abounds with fish.
CAVALLY (LITTLE), a river of Upper Guinea, on the Grain coast, which flows into the Atlantic at Cape Town, about 10 m. ENE of Cape Palmas.
CAVALOS (Angra-Dos), a bay on the W coast of Sudan, 140 m. SSW of Cape Bojador, in N lat. 24° 15'.

CAVAN, an inland county of Ireland, the most southerly of Ulster, and forming, to a great extent, a southern projection of that prov. It is bounded on the N by Leitrim, Fermanagh, and Monaghan; on the NE by Monaghan; on the E and SE by Meath; on the S by Meath, Westmeath, and Long ford; and on the W by Longford and Leitrim. Its outline is irregular; yet may be viewed as a curved oval, sending off a small NW projection. The county extends from NW to SE across the interior of the narrowest part of the kingdom; and reaches from within 21 m. of the Atlantic ocean, at Donegal bay, to within 181 m. of the Irish sea, at the bay of Dundalk. Its greatest length, from NE to SW, is 40 m.; its greatest breadth, in the opposite direction, is 22\frac{1}{2} m.; and its area comprises 375,473 acres of arable land, 71,918 of uncultivated land, 7,325 of plantations, 502 of towns, and 22,142 of water,-in all 477,360 acres. All the NW projection, comprising an area of about 84 sq. m., is a congeries of uplands, and prevailingly rugged, bleak, wild, and lofty. Several hills, in single masses, or in moderately prolonged ridges, occur S of Ballyconnel, along the E border, and in some parts of the interior; but they are little more than sufficient to give a generally undulated or partially broken appearance to a prevailing expanse of cold champaign country, often bleak in aspect, and freely interspersed with bog and naked waste or pasture. Waters.] Loughs Cane and Nitty touch the N boundary of the NW projection. The Cloddagh rivulet runs across the neck or isthmus of the projection. The Woodford, the draining stream of the SE district of co. Leitrim, forms the pleasant lakes of Woodford on the borders of Leitrim and Cavan, and traverses the latter co. 10 m. NE to the Erne. Lough Gawnagh, 3 m. in length, extends along the Sboundary. The Upper Erne, issuing from this lake, runs 20 m. nearly due N, right across the co., past Belturbet, to the head of Upper Lough Erne. This river, at about the middle of its career in the co., expands into Lough Oughter. Lough Shillin, 43 m. in length, and nearly 2 m. in breadth, extends along the S boundary to within 51 m. of the nearest part of Lough Gawnagh. Two head-streams of the Blackwater, each about 11 m. in length, rise within less than 3 m. of each other, and about the same distance from the NE boundary of the county; both run S and SSE to Lough Ramor; and after traversing that lake of about 3 m. in length, re-appear in the single stream of the Blackwater, which almost immediately passes into Meath. The Annalee and the Cootehill rivulets come in from Monaghan, at points respectively 3\frac{1}{4} and 7\frac{1}{4} m. N of Shircock; they unite at a point 3½ m. W of Cootehill; and the united stream runs W to the Erne. Minerals.] Granite occupies a space of about 5 or 6 sq. m. immediately NW of Ballynenagh. Clayslate, greywacke, greywacke slate, and other modifications of hard schistose rock of the Wernerian primitive and transition classes, occupy by far the greater part of the county. A formation of millstone grit, white sandstone, shale, and thin strata of limestone and coal, occupies the greater part of the NW projection of the county. Mountain or carboniferous limestone, with its accompanying strata, occupies about 11 sq. m. in the extreme NW, or inward from the shores of the Loughs Macnean. Coal is worked on the extreme western boundary; iron is mined among the uplands in the vicinity of Swanlinbar; lead and copper occur in the neighbourhood of Cootehill; lead and silver ores have been discovered near Ballyconnel; and ochres and coarse manganese are comparatively abundant, and somewhat extensively diffused. The limestone of the county appears to be but limitedly available for economical purposes. Marl, potter's clay, brick clay, and fuller's earth, are plentiful. Agriculture.] The baronies of Upper and Lower ょうない ストリップ ナイラグ サイン サイン・アイ・アイ・アイ・アイ・アイ・アイ・アイ・アイ・アイ・アイ・アイ・アイ・アイ・ Loughtee-which include all the lower parts of the valleys of the Erne, the Woodford, and the Annalee, as well as part of the central third of the valley of the Erne, may be fairly selected as furnishing at least an average specimen of the agricultural condition and practices of the county. Yet a well-accredited report on these baronies, which was published in 1836, and now lies before us, depicts the state of agriculture in this co. in dismal colours. "Agriculture," says this document, "is in the most wretched state imaginable; no draining or improvement is going on." Most of the landlords are absentees; and no example or encouragement is given to the tenants. The consequence is, they and the land are impoverished. The land is mostly cultivated with the spade. The plough, when used, is frequently a rude instrument made by the farmer himself. Yet many patches, and even belts, and expanses of the county display a vigour of soil, and luxuriance of produce, which might cheat a stranger into the opinion that the whole co. is prosperous. In 1847, the extent of ground under corn and beans was 107,876 acres, of which 99,753 acres were oats. The ground under potatoes and green crops was 12,878 acres; under flax 1,625 acres; in clover and meadow grass, 35,987 acres. The amount of live stock in 1847 was 9,685 horses; 8,599 asses; 79,718 horned cattle; 9,892 sheep; 13,136 pigs; 7,693 goats; and 182,885 head of poultry.-In 1841 the number of farms in the co., from 1 acre to 5 acres, was 10,878; from 5 to 15 acres, 12,231; from 15 to 30 acres, 1,959; and upwards of 30 acres, 669; -and of these, 71 of the first class, 23 of the second, 1 of the third, and 1 of the fourth, were in the civic districts. In 1847 the total number of holdings was 27,161, of which 3,895 were under 5 acres; 13,022 under 15 acres; 6,507 under 30 acres; and 2,562 above 30 acres.—In 1841, the plantations within the county consisted of 7,325 acres; and the number of detached trees was 326,988, equivalent to 2,044 acres, —making the grand total of woods 9,369 acres. Trade.] The weaving and bleaching of linen is the only manufacture of considerable value. The bleaching establishments, in 1802, were of such aggregate extent as to be capable of employing a capital of £45,500, and bleaching at a time 91,000 webs. The linen trade has experienced prolonged and comparatively great declension. Other manufactures and all the commerce of the co. may, with slight exceptions, be summed up in breweries, distilleries, and exchange of farm and dairy produce for scanty supplies of British manufactures and colonial goods.— The principal lines of road through the county are four in number, and all traverse it in directions between N by W and NW by N. One enters from the SE, and passes through Kingscourt, Shircock, and Cootehill, towards Clones; the second enters 5½ m. W of the former, passes through Bailieborough, and joins the former a little S of Cootehill; the third enters a little E of Lough Ramor, passes through Virginia, Ballyjamesduff, and Cavan, sends off branches to Belturbet and toward Clones, and departs in the direction of Enniskillen; and the fourth enters between Loughs Ramor and Sheelan, passes through Ballynenagh, Killeshandra, and Swanlinbar, and departs to ramify itself toward Enniskillen and Sligo. The only navigation enjoyed by the county is down the Erne from Belturbet to Lough Erne, and thence, through the Ulster canal, to the ramified water-communications of the north-east of the kingdom.—It has been proposed to form a railway commencing on the N side of the city of Dublin, and pursuing a NW direction by the towns of Castleknock, Dunboyne, Dunshaughlin, Kilmessan, Trim, Navan, Athboy, Kells, Virginia, Ballyjamesduff, and **** Shadone, to terminate for the present at Cavan, distant from Dublin about 68 m. Divisions and Towns.] The chief towns and villages, with their respective pop. in 1831, were, -in Castleraghan, Ballyjamesduff, 863, Kilnaleck, 347, Virginia, 930, and Mullogh, 108;-in Clonkee, Bailieborough, 1,085, Kingscourt, 1,616, and Shircock, 348; -in Clonmahon, Ballynenagh, 702, and Mount- Nugent, 171; -in Lower Loughtee, Belturbet, 2,026; -in Upper Loughtee, Ballyhaise, 761, Butler's- Bridge, 211, Stradone, 250, and Cavan, 2,931;-in Tullaghagh, Swanlinbar, 398, Ballymagauran, 89, Kilsub, 60, and Ballyconnel, 453; -in Tullaghgarvey, Clementstown, 182, Ballinacargy, 150, and Cootehill, 2,239; -and in Tullaghonoho, Killeshandra, 1,137, Arvagh, 422, and Scrabby, 183. Excepting two small sections on the S and E, the whole county is in the dio. of Kilmore, and constitutes very nearly all its extent. Statistics.] The annual valuation of the county under the poor-law acts in 1841 was £248,115 8s. 9d. The estimated rental to proprietors is £101,890; and the estimated value of landed produce, £1,204,000. In 1847, the valuation to poor-law was £274,754.— The county returns two members to parliament. The constituency at the close of 1840 amounted to 2,050; and consisted of 263 £50 freeholders, 140 £20 freeholders, 1,506 £10 freeholders, 19 £20 leaseholders, 115 £10 leaseholders, and 7 £10 rent-chargers. On 1st Feb. 1847, the constituency amounted to only 1,492, of whom 256 were £50 freeholders; and 1,052, £10 freeholders. Pop. in 1831, 227,933; in 1841, 243,158. All the following statistics are those of 1841. Males, 120,814; females, 122,344; families, 42,592. Inhabited houses, 40,964; uninhabited built houses, 1,349; houses in the course of erection, 70. Families residing in first class houses, 455; in second class houses, 7,402; in third class houses, 20,107; in fourth class houses, 14,628. Families employed chiefly in agriculture, 31,887; in manufactures and trade, 8,338; in other pursuits, 2,367. Families dependent chiefly on vested means and professions, 780; on the directing of labour, 12,232; on their own manual labour, 28,837; on means not specified, 743. History.] At the time of the Conquest, the territory which now constitutes the county of Cavan was called Breffiny, Briefny, or Brenny, and was held by the clan O'Reilly. In the reign of Elizabeth it was erected into a sheriffdom, and divided into 5 baronies, which were assigned to different heads or chiefs of the O'Reilly sept. In 1610, when six other counties in Ulster were forfeited by the rebellion of O'Neill and O'Donnel, and when the great design became adopted of a general 'plantation' of Ulster, Cavan, which might otherwise have been permitted to remain unchallenged in the possession of the O'Reillys, was transmuted into the seat of a great British colony, upwards of 52,000 English acres being taken from the natives, and about 38,000 of these distributed among colonists. Castles were now built on the estates of all the heads of the colony, the towns of Belturbet, Virginia, and Ballyconnel, and numerous villages were founded.

CAVAN, a post and market town, the cap. of the above co., and formerly a parliamentary borough, in the p. of Urney, on the mail-road from Dublin to Enniskillen, and on a tributary of the Annalee river, 3½ m. E of Lough Oughter, 25 m. SSE of Enniskillen. Nearly the whole town, with the exception of a new street and the public buildings, is of unpretending character, and thickly daubed with rows of mere cabins. Yet it presents little or none of the abject meanness which characterises so many towns of its size in the W and S of Ireland. The Charter schoolhouse, or 'College,' as it is locally called, stands a little S of the town, and is a modern and commodious building, erected in 1819 at a cost of £9,000. The court-house, situated in the open space about 60 yards W of the parish-church, and erected at a cost of £11,000, is a graceful structure, after a design by Bowden. The barracks is a permanent military station. The gaol, situated 300 yards S of the courthouse, presents a striking appearance of both site and structure, and contains 68 cells and 16 other rooms. The town has a share, somewhat proportioned to its pop., in the linen trade of the county. Its general trade, through the medium of its markets and fairs, was formerly inconsiderable, but has increased since tolls and customs ceased to be collected. Its retail trade is of comparatively large importance, and commands a large extent of surrounding country. Pop. in 1831, 2,931; in 1841, 3,749. Houses 522. Families employed chiefly in agriculture, 150; in manufactures and trade, 341; in other pursuits, 199.
CAVAN, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Cotes-du-Nord, cant. of La Roche-Derrien, 7 m. SE of Lannion. Pop. 1,831.
CAVANELLA, a town of Venetian Lombardy, on the l. bank of the Adige, 4 m. SW of its entrance into the Adriatic, and 22 m. SSW of Venice.

CAVANELLA-DI-PO, a canal of Venetian Lombardy, which issues from the Po 5 m. SE of Adria, runs 13 m. NW along the confines of the prov. of the Adriatic, and unites with the canal Bianco. It forms, with the canal of Loreo, a communication between the Adige and Po.
CAVANILLAS, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and SW of Soria.

CAVARZERE, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the delegation and 25 m. SSW of Venice, and 12 m. SW of Chioggia, on the Adige, by which it is divided into two parts, named from their relative position C. Destro and C. Sinistro. Pop. 7,000. This town has considerable commerce in cattle, silk, wood, grain, and forage.
CAVASO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the circle and 29 m. NW of Treviso. Pop. 2,500. It possesses manufactories of cloth, linen, hats, and dyestuffs.
CAVAURY, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Para, which inosculates with the Hyucara and Amazon.

CAVA - ZUCCHERINA, or CAVA - ZUCCARINA (LA), a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. and 18 m. NE of Venice, district and 8 m. SE of Santa-Dona, at the junction of an old arm of the Piave with the canal of the same name.
CAVAZZO, a village in the duchy and 12 m. NNE of Modena, and 4 m. SE of La Mirandola. Pop. 1,400.
CAVE (NORTH), a parish and township of the E. R. of Yorkshire, 63 m. SSE of Market-Weighton. Area 6,360 acres. Pop. 1,217. Area of t. 3,270 acres. Pop. 897.
CAVE (South), a parish and township of the E. R. of Yorkshire, 17 m. SE of Market-Weighton. Area 7,480 acres. Pop. 1,852. Area of t. 4,630 acres. Pop. 1,288.
CAVENAGO, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. of Lodi-e-Crema, district and 7 m. NE of Borghetto, near the r. bank of the Adda. Pop. 1,037.
CAVENDISH, a parish of Suffolk, 23 m. E of Clare, bounded on the S by the Stour. Area 3,450 acres. Pop. 1,353.
CAVENDISH, a township of Windsor co., in the state of Vermont, U. S., 74 m. S of Montpelier, watered by Black river and Twenty Mile stream. Pop. in 1840, 1,427.
CAVENHAM, a parish of Suffolk, 41 m. SSE of Mildenhall, and bounded on the N by the Lack. Area 2,630 acres. Pop. 277.
CAVERN ISLAND, an island of the South Atlantic ocean, in Prince Edward's group, in S lat. 47°, E long. 38°.
CAVERNAES, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, 5 m. ENE of Viseu. CAV

CAVERS, a parish in Roxburghshire, consisting of two separate portions, both lying on the S side of the Teviot. In the W division the Wisp rises to an alt. of 1,830 ft. above sea-level. In the N division is the v. of Denholm. Pop. of the p. in 1801, 1,382; in 1841, 1,709.
CAVERSFIELD, a parish of Oxfordshire, 13 m. N of Bicester. Area 1,200 acres. Pop. 178.
CAVERSHAM, a parish of Oxfordshire, 1 m. N of Reading. Area 5,100 acres. Pop. 1,642.
CAVERSWALL, a parish of Staffordshire, 4 m. W of Cheadle, intersected by a branch of the Trent. Area 5,380 acres. Pop. 1,505.
CAVERTITZ, a village of Saxony, in the circle of Nieissen, NW of Strehla. Pop. 296.

CAVERTON, a village in the p. of Eckford, in Roxburghshire, 43 m. S of Kelso. Pop. 50. CAVERY, CAUVERY, or KAVERI, a river of Hindostan, which takes its rise in the Western Ghauts, in the Koorg country, at an alt. of 4,000 ft. above sea-level; runs in a circuitous course between banks richly clothed with forests, until it enters the prov. of Mysore; at Cananore, bends ESE; receives the Hennavutty on the 1.; passes Seringapatam, and is joined by the Cabbany on the r., and on the l., on the confines of the prov., by the Shinsha and Arkavutty. Thence it directs its course SSE, forming the line of separation between the prov. of Coimbatur and Salem, and receives on the r. the Bovary, Noil, and Ambraddy. After the junction of the last river it takes an ESE direction, and on reaching the plains of the Carnatic divides about 10 m. above Trichinopoly into two branches, one of which, taking the name of Colerun, runs NE and falls into the bay of Bengal at Porto-Novo; the other retaining the original appellation, subdivides into numerous branches, forming the delta in which Tranquebar, Carricall, &c., are situated. It has a total course of about 420 m. The C. is not generally fordable. Its smallest depth may be 2 or 2½ ft., but the greater portion is much beyond this; and from May to November it cannot be forded in any part of its course from a short distance below Bajamandla: it is indeed swollen by the rains of both monsoons, but chiefly by those of the SW. The island of Seringapatam is formed in the C. by its separating into two branches, which meet again, and enclose an island 31 m. long, and 1 m. broad. The waters of the river were particularly low when the storming party crossed its rocky bed on the 4th May, 1799, and carried the breach in the fortress at the NW angle of the island. A few hours afterwards, the C. swelled so much from the rains as even to cover the breaching-batteries on the opposite side, and continued in that state for some months. The established passages over the C. are few in number. The only safe passage at all seasons of the year is by the bridges which lead over the island of Seringapatam. At Sisasammudram, 40 m. from Mysore -where the river divides into two branches and descends in two separate falls of 200 ft. and 300 ft.—a magnificent bridge, 1,000 ft. in length, by 13 ft. in breadth, and 23 ft. in height, supported on 400 pillars of stone, was erected across the N branch in 1819-21 by a private individual.
CAVERYPATAM, a village of Hindostan, in the Carnatic, 6 m. N of Tranquebar, near the mouth of the New Cavery.

CAVERYPAUK, a town of Hindostan, in the Carnatic, 57 m. WSW of Madras, near the S bank of the Palar. It is neatly built, and is still surrounded by an old mud wall. Its fort, which was formerly one of considerable strength, is now in ruins. The locality is chiefly remarkable for a tank, the most magnificent structure of its class in the south of India. It is 8 m. in length, and 3 m. in breadth; and enclosed by an embankment 30 ft. in height, faced with large stones, and planted on the outside with palmira trees. CAVERYPORAM, CAVERIPURA, or COVIPORAM, a town of Hindostan, in the Carnatic, in the prov. and 89 m. NE of Coimbatur, on the r. bank of the Cavery, which is here occasionally fordable during the summer months. C. is a considerable entrepot for the trade of the E coast.
CAVEZZANA D'ANTENA, a parish and village of Tuscany, in the comp. of Pisa, in the Val-di-Magra, 9 m. NNE of Pontremoli. Pop. 206. Mines of copper exist here.
CAVI, or CAVE, a town in the States - of - the- Church, 26 m. ESE of Rome, and 3 m. E of Pa- 623. lestrina. Pop. 2,000.
CAVI (ROCCA DI), a village 3 m. distant from the town of Cavi in the States-of-the-Church, on a high table-land of the Guadagnolo mountain.

CAVIANA, an island of Brazil, in the estuary of the Amazon, to the N of the island of Marajó, and separated from the continent on the W by the canal of Braganza. Its greatest breadth is 13 m.; its cir- 1,108. cumf. 28 m. It contains a small town, named Robordello, now nearly depopulated, and to the NE of the town a large rural establishment, called the Fazenda-da-Caridade, belonging to an hospital in Belem. The surface of this island, which is flat, is to a great extent covered with macacu trees. The soil is fertile, and affords excellent pasturage.
CAVICUNA, a town in the island of Luzon, in the Asiatic archipelago, on the N coast.
CAVIGNAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Gironde, cant. of Saint-Savin. Pop. 715.
CAVILLARGUES, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Gard, cant. of Bagnols, 10 m. NE of Uzis. Pop. 846.
CAVILLI, an inlet of the Asiatic archipelago, in the Mindoro sea, between the islands of Palawan and Negros, in N lat. 9° 15', E long. 120° 45'. It belongs to Britain.
CAVIOLA (CAPE), a headland on the coast of the isle of Palma, in the Canary group.
CAVITAL, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. and 85 m. SE of Bejapore.

CAVITE, or CABITE, a town and port of the island of Luzon, Asiatic archipelago, on the bay and 8 m. S of Manila. Pop. 5,000, chiefly Spaniards. It is situated on a tongue of land which encloses the harbour on the SW, and is defended by the fort of San- Felipe. It possesses an extensive military depot, an arsenal, and building-docks, and contains 2 churches and 3 convents. This town was formerly very flourishing, and is still the most important fortress and one of the principal commercial entrepots belonging to Spain in the group of the Philippines. Perouse determined its position to be in N lat. 14° 29′ 9″, E long. 118° 50′ 40″.
CAVOLI, a small island near the SE extremity of the island of Sardinia, 4 m. SE of Cape Carbonara. It is the ancient Ficaria.
CAVO (MONTE). See ALBANO.
CAVONE. See SALANDRELLA.

CAVOR, a town of Piedmont, in the prov. and 7 m. SE of Pinerolo, and 25 m. SW of Turin. Pop. 6,000. It is situated in a fertile plain, at the base of a ramification of the Alps, and contains a communal college. It possesses manufactories of linen, tanneries, and silk-spinning-mills, and has a considerable trade in grain. Fairs are held twice a-year. In 1808 it was greatly damaged by an earthquake. The environs contain extensive quarries of marble and slate; and at a short distance from the town is the once wealthy abbey of Santa Maria-di-Cavor. CAVOSESTO, CAPOCESTO, or CAOSESTO, a town | E side of the Western Andes, at an alt. of 9,000 ft. $ CAX and port of Austria, in the prov. of Dalmatia, circle and 28 m. WNW of Spalatro, on the Adriatic, in N lat. 43° 35'. It is enclosed by old walls, and is illbuilt. The harbour is entered by two narrow channels formed by an island which lies at its mouth.
CAVOY, a river of Hindostan, which, after flowin a N course parallel to the Canara coast, and very near to the sea, discharges itself 4 m. N of Mount Dilly, in N lat. 11° 59'.
CAVRIA, a town of Austria, in the Tyrol, prov. and 26 m. WSW of Belluno, on the l. side of the Canal Bovo.
